data.title: Congress closer to government shutdown after Senate blocks funding bill
data.description:
Senate Democrats and a few Republicans on Thursday blocked a major government funding package that needs to pass by midnight Saturday to avoid a government shutdown, signaling that negotiations between President Trump and Senate Democrats still have a way to go before reaching a deal.

A motion to advance the bill failed by a vote of 45-55. 

Eight Republicans joined all Democrats in voting against it, including Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.). Thune was a late “no” vote and immediately entered a motion to reconsider the package to give him flexibility to bring it back to the floor soon. 

Democrats blocked the funding package, which includes six bills, because it includes funding for the Department of Homeland Security.

They say they will not support Homeland Security funding until the Trump administration agrees to reforms to rein in Immigration and Customs Enforcement’s (ICE) large-scale immigration crackdown and puts an end to violent encounters between federal law enforcement and protesters.

Sen. Patty Murray (D-Wash.), the vice chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee, who played a major role in negotiating the spending package, announced on the Senate floor Thursday morning that she would vote “no” because the White House had still not agreed to a deal to separate Homeland Security funding from the bill.

She said that Democrats would be “very happy” to pass the five non-Homeland Security funding measures but declared that any package that includes the Homeland Security title is a non-starter with her and other Democrats.

“We are all in agreement on funding for child care, cancer research, air traffic controllers, our troops and more but we have also been clear that we cannot and will not move the DHS bill without real progress on accountability,” she said on the Senate floor.

“ICE and CBP are out of control,” she added, referring to Customs and Border Protection.

There was some optimism in the Senate Thursday morning that the package might advance after Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and Trump’s team made some progress on Wednesday toward potentially splitting off the Homeland Security bill and setting it aside for future reforms.  

A source familiar with the discussions told The Hill that while no agreement had been reached yet, the discussions were ongoing and moving in Democrats’ direction.

Even so, that progress wasn’t enough to convince Murray and other Democrats to vote to advance the funding package.

“While I am very glad to see the White House and Republicans are now talking with Democrats to finalize a plan to pass the five bills and split off DHS, until that deal is finalized, I will be a no on this vote,” Murray announced on the floor.

In a surprising development. Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), the centrist Democrat who repeatedly criticized his party in the fall for forcing a 43-day government shutdown, also voted “no” on advancing the bill.

A source familiar with Fetterman’s thinking said he voted against advancing the six-bill package with the expectation that blocking the package on the floor would set the stage for unbundling the Homeland Security appropriations measure from five other spending bills. 

Fetterman called on Trump Tuesday to fire Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem.

Seven fiscally conservative Republican senators also voted against the measure.

Sens. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), Ron Johnson (R-Wis.), Mike Lee (R-Utah), Ashley Moody (R-Fla.), Rand Paul (R-Ky.), Rick Scott (R-Fla.) and Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.) also voted against proceeding to the bill.

data.title: Appeals court finds Noem illegally ended deportation protections for Venezuela, Haiti
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem acted unlawfully when she lifted deportation protections for Venezuela and Haiti, a three-judge panel of the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeal ruled late Wednesday.

A concurring opinion noted that both Noem and President Trump repeatedly made statements that “were overtly founded on racist stereotyping based on country of origin,” agreeing with a lower court that the decision to do so was “preordained” and not based on need.

The ruling will have little effect for Venezuelan citizens. A Supreme Court ruling in October allowed Noem’s bid to end Temporary Protected Status (TPS) while the larger court battle continued.

“The Secretary’s unlawful actions have had real and significant consequences for the hundreds of thousands of Venezuelans and Haitians in the United States who rely on TPS. The record is replete with examples of hard-working, contributing members of society—who are mothers, fathers, wives, husbands, and partners of U.S. citizens, pay taxes, and have no criminal records—who have been deported or detained after losing their TPS,” Judge Kim McLane Wardlaw wrote in the opinion.

“The Secretary’s actions have left hundreds of thousands of people in a constant state of fear that they will be deported, detained, separated from their families, and returned to a country in which they were subjected to violence or any other number of harms.”

The judges found Noem cannot simply “vacate” the earlier TPS designations for Venezuela and Haiti and instead must follow the process for doing so, including analyzing conditions in each country.

A concurring opinion by Judge Salvador Mendoza also dug into Noem and Trump’s comments about citizens of both countries, arguing there was “ample evidence of racial and national origin animus in the record, which reinforces the district court’s conclusion that the Secretary’s actions were preordained and her reasoning pretextual.”

“We cannot ignore the backdrop of extraordinary statements by direct decision-makers when assessing whether the agency’s proffered rationale was genuine or merely a pretext for an ulterior (and impermissible) motive. The record is replete with public statements by Secretary Noem and President Donald Trump that evince a hostility toward, and desire to rid the country of, TPS holders who are Venezuelan and Haitian,” he wrote.

“Animus based on race or national origin can never qualify as a ‘political consideration[]’ or ‘Administration priorit[y].’”

A federal judge in Washington is expected to rule any day on a request to pause the termination of TPS for Haiti while a separate lawsuit challenging it proceeds. The country’s TPS designation is scheduled to end on February 3.

Temporary Protected Status, or TPS, authorized by Congress as part of the Immigration Act of 1990, allows the Homeland Security secretary to grant legal immigration status to people fleeing countries experiencing civil strife, environmental disaster or other “extraordinary and temporary conditions” that prevent a safe return to that home country.

Designations are granted for terms of six, 12 or 18 months, and extensions can be granted so long as conditions remain dire. The status prevents holders from being deported and allows them to work, but it does not give them a path to citizenship.

Millions of Venezuelans have fled political unrest, mass unemployment and hunger. The country is mired in a prolonged crisis brought on by years of hyperinflation, political corruption, economic mismanagement and an ineffectual government.

Haiti was first designated for TPS in 2010 after a catastrophic magnitude 7.0 earthquake killed and wounded hundreds of thousands of people, and left more than 1 million homeless. Haitians face widespread hunger and gang violence.

The Associated Press Contributed.

data.title: Iran planning live fire drills in Strait of Hormuz amid tensions with US
data.description:
Iran is preparing live fire drills in the Strait of Hormuz following tensions ratcheting up between Tehran and the U.S, according to Iranian state media.

In a post on the social platform X on Thursday, Iranian state television’s English-language arm Press TV said that naval forces with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) “will carry out live-fire exercises next week in the Strait of Hormuz.”

On Wednesday, President Trump warned Iran of a “far worse” attack than three strikes last summer.

“Hopefully Iran will quickly ‘Come to the Table’ and negotiate a fair and equitable deal – NO NUCLEAR WEAPONS – one that is good for all parties. Time is running out, it is truly of the essence!” Trump said on Truth Social.

“As I told Iran once before, MAKE A DEAL! They didn’t, and there was ‘Operation Midnight Hammer,’ a major destruction of Iran. The next attack will be far worse! Don’t make that happen again. Thank you for your attention to this matter!” he added.

Also on Wednesday, Iran’s mission to the United Nations warned of a response “like never before” after Trump’s Truth Social threat.

“Last time the U.S. blundered into wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, it squandered over $7 trillion and lost more than 7,000 American lives,” the Iranian mission wrote on X.

“Iran stands ready for dialogue based on mutual respect and interests—BUT IF PUSHED, IT WILL DEFEND ITSELF AND RESPOND LIKE NEVER BEFORE!” it added.

An activist group said Tuesday that over 6,000 people have been killed during Tehran’s crackdown on recent protests in Iran. The protests started in late December due to the falling value of Iran’s currency.

Iran’s government responded to the demonstrations with a historically bloody crackdown and a two-week internet blackout.

data.title: Tillis on Homan news conference: Noem, Miller 'take notes'
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) on Thursday commended border czar Tom Homan for conducting a news conference in Minnesota on immigration enforcement and told Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller to “take notes.”

“This is what competent leadership looks like. Noem and Miller, take notes,” he wrote in a post on the social platform X.

President Trump sent Homan in to quell outrage following the killing of two U.S. citizens at the hands of immigration authorities. 

Homan told reporters, “I didn’t come to Minnesota for photo ops or headlines. You haven’t seen me. I came here to seek solutions, and that’s what we’re going to do.”

“And we’ve come a long way, and we got some good wins for the people of Minnesota, I think, and for the administration and for the safety security of this city. One thing we did agree on, though, everybody I talked to agreed on, was that community safety is paramount,” he added.

Tillis has railed against Miller for labeling 37-year-old ICU nurse Alex Pretti a “domestic terrorist” prior to an investigation into his death at the hands of a Border Patrol agent. Noem similarly labeled local Minneapolis resident Renee Good, killed earlier this month by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer, a “domestic terrorist” prior to an investigation, sparking backlash.

“Stephen Miller never fails to live up to my expectations of incompetence,” Tillis told reporters Tuesday. 

In response to the criticism, Trump said Tillis and Alaska Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R), who both called for Noem’s removal, are “losers.”

“They’re terrible senators. One is gone and the other should be gone,” the president told reporters. 

The North Carolina senator laughed at the comment.

“I am thrilled about that. That makes me qualified to be [Department of Homeland Security] secretary and senior advisor to the president,” Tillis told CNN’s Manu Raju on Capitol Hill, referring to Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller.

data.title: Assailant convicted after Barron Trump notifies London police of crime
data.description:
A 22-year-old Russian man has been found guilty of assaulting a woman in connection with an incident that Barron Trump, President Trump’s youngest son, told London police he witnessed on a video call last year, according to British media reports.

Matvei Rumiantsev was convicted in an east London court on Wednesday of assaulting the woman in January 2025. He was found not guilty of one count of rape and intentional strangulation relating to the same incident, and another count of rape and assault stemming from a separate alleged November 2024 incident, The Independent reported.

Rumiantsev’s trial took an unexpected turn last week when the victim, who cannot be named for legal reasons, told the court that a phone call from Trump “helped save [her] life.”

Prosecutors reportedly said that Rumiantsev was “jealous” of her friendship with Trump, which he later admitted.

Jurors subsequently heard a recording of that Jan. 18, 2025, phone call in which Trump, 19, said to a London police operator he had picked a video call from the woman and saw she was being attacked.

“I’m calling from the U.S., uh I just got a call from a girl…she’s getting beat up,” Trump said, according to an official transcript released by the Crown Prosecution Service.

“This was happening about eight minutes ago. I just figured out how to, how to call someone. Uh, uh it’s really an emergency,” he continued.

The operator asked for information about the victim, including her name and age, and asked how Trump knew her.

“I mean these details don’t matter. She’s getting beat up,” Trump responded.  

Asked how he knew what happened, Trump replied that he “got a call from her with a guy beating her up.” The operator continued to press Trump on how he knew the victim.

“I don’t think these details matter. She’s getting beat up but okay fine, also I met her on social media, I don’t think that matters,” Trump said, repeating “she’s getting beat up.”

The operator then asked Trump to “stop being rude and actually answer my questions.”

“If you want to help the person, you’ll answer my questions clearly and precisely, thank you. So how do you know her?” the operator asked again.

Trump replied, “I met her on social media.”

Trump said he did not know the name of the alleged attacker and repeatedly emphasized the urgency of the situation.

“She’s getting really badly beat up and the call was about eight minutes ago, I don’t know what could have happened by now,” he said, saying he was “sorry for being rude.”

Rumiantsev is set to be sentenced in March.

data.title: Cruz aide bats down talk of Nicki Minaj photo frustration
data.description:
A top aide for Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) dismissed a Wednesday report that the senator was upset with the White House over not getting a chance to take a photo with Nicki Minaj.

Macarena Martinez, Cruz’s communications director, posted a photo on social platform X of Cruz, White House adviser Alex Bruesewitz and Minaj. Politico’s Sophia Cai reported earlier Wednesday that the Texas Republican’s team was “not happy” with Bruesewitz after he did not get the chance to take a photo with Minaj. 

“Not sure who your source is, what their motive is (or yours), or why you’re so eager to get spun up and used,” Martinez wrote. “But here’s everyone looking so upset! Try again.”

On Thursday morning, Bruesewitz wrote on X that “many people in D.C. have reached out” about the reported frustrations Cruz’s team had with him. 

“There was nothing personal & I hope people aren’t actually upset with me,” he added. “But who knows? We were in a hurry trying to get [Minaj] into the Oval Office to see our great President.”

“She didn’t have time to stop and take pictures with everyone,” Bruesewitz continued. “She was incredibly kind and gracious with her time backstage with as many people as humanly possible. She took dozens of pictures with fans.”

Minaj was in the nation’s capital to tout her investment in the Trump administration’s new child investment accounts, dubbed “Trump accounts.” The Trinidadian rapper later said she received a “gold card” visa, which typically costs $1 million, from the administration for free.

During a Wednesday event promoting the initiative, the president said that Minaj will contribute “hundreds of thousands of dollars” into the accounts of her fans. Details about the exact amount, as well as who qualifies, are unclear.

Minaj, who joined Trump onstage at the event, called herself “probably the president’s “No. 1 fan.” 

“And that’s not going to change,” she noted. “And the hate or what people have to say, it does not affect me at all. It actually motivates me to support him more, and it’s going to motivate all of us to support him more.”

Minaj also spoke briefly with House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.), who quipped that he “almost wore” the same beige fur coat she donned.

“I’m so glad I didn’t,” Johnson remarked.

data.title: Live updates: Trump holds Cabinet meeting; Homan says Minneapolis operation will be 'by the book'
data.description:
President Trump assembled his Cabinet for a meeting on Thursday, touting his adminstration’s achievements after a year in office.

Trump administration border czar Tom Homan said at a press conference Thursday morning that the immigration crackdown in Minneapolis will be done “by the book” as President Trump looks to “fix” it.

“I do not want to hear that everything that has been done here has been perfect. Nothing is ever perfect. Anything can be improved on. What we’ve been working on is making this operation safer, more efficient, by the book,” Homan said Thursday.

The Senate is also scrambling as a Jan. 30 partial shutdown deadline quickly approaches and Democrats dig their heels in on Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding.

After Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by immigration officials in Minneapolis, the fight over President Trump’s immigration enforcement operations has reached a fever pitch across the country and on Capitol Hill. Some Senate Republicans, however, have said they are open to splitting off the DHS funding from a six-bill package to avoid another shutdown.

Trump later Thursday will join first lady Melania Trump at the premiere of her documentary at the Kennedy Center.

Follow live below.

data.title: Bessent: Instead of buying toys for kids, contribute to 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Wednesday that relatives of children should donate to their new “Trump accounts” as opposed to buying them a toy. 

“Relatives, rather than giving a toy for a birthday, for a holiday, they can contribute to these accounts,” Bessent said on “Fox and Friends.” The Treasury secretary also called contributions to the accounts a “new kind of philanthropy.”

Bessent and President Trump touted the tax-free accounts, also called Section 530A accounts, during a Wednesday summit. Established under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that Trump signed into law last July, the accounts are available for children who do not turn 18 before the end of the calendar year in which their parents open an account, according to the IRS.

Additionally, Treasury is offering to contribute $1,000 to accounts for children born from Jan. 1, 2025, through Dec. 31, 2028. Starting July 5, individuals — including family members, friends and other adults — can contribute up to $5,000 per year to an account, a cap that will increase with inflation starting in 2028. Employers of the child or their parent can also contribute up to $2,500 annually, which counts against the $5,000 limit.

Charitable organizations and state governments may also make contributions, which do not count against the annual limit. 

The funds in the account have to be invested in certain mutual funds or exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500 or “another index of primarily American equities,” the IRS says.

Parents can open an account for their child via elections on IRS Form 4547. Their children can access the funds in their account on Jan. 1 of the year they turn 18.

During the summit, Bessent said the accounts “will turn simple investments into life-changing money” that children can use to pay for college, buy their first home or save for retirement.

“Trump accounts are a generational downpayment on the American dream,” he added.

The White House Council of Economic Advisers (CEA) projected in August that, under average stock market conditions, accounts for babies born this year will grow to more than $5,800 by the time they turn 18, without contributions. Under the same stock market conditions but with maximum contributions, those accounts will accrue to more than $303,000 in value, CEA projects.

data.title: Walz: Trump compared Minnesota crackdown to Venezuela operation
data.description:
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) on Wednesday said President Trump compared his federal immigration crackdown in the North Star State to the U.S. military operation in Venezuela that ousted President Nicolás Maduro.

“He told me that he doesn’t understand what’s wrong with Minnesota,” Walz told MS NOW’s Jacob Soboroff, recalling his call with Trump on Monday. “‘I don’t know what’s wrong with you people.’ I said,’ Nothing’s wrong with us, Mr. President. We’re one of the most effective states and one of the best places to live.'”

“And he told me that, ‘Well, look, Tim, we did this in New Orleans. We did it in Louisville. There’s no problems,'” Walz continued. “And I said, ‘You didn’t kill anybody in Louisville or New Orleans. And the operation here looks very different from that.’ And and then he told me it was successful in Venezuela.”

Soboroff pressed on Trump’s comparison, to which Walz said he was unsure why, with “what’s happening to my state, that I’m interested in Venezuela.”

“But he told me how well that went,” the Minnesota governor added. “Which really was strange to me was he saw an operation in Venezuela against a foreign nation in the same context he saw an operation against a U.S. state and a U.S. city.”

Walz told Soboroff that Trump’s call was about “needing something from us,” and that Trump called because “his poll numbers dropped and it looked bad on TV.” The governor added that Trump never mentioned the names of Alex Pretti or Renee Good, two U.S. citizens killed during federal immigration operations in Minneapolis this month.

The Hill reached out to the White House for comment.

Trump said he and Walz had a “very good call,” which was requested after a second U.S. citizen, Alex Pretti, was killed after an altercation with federal officers, fueling anger with the administration’s aggressive immigration crackdown.

“I told Governor Walz that I would have Tom Homan call him, and that what we are looking for are any and all Criminals that they have in their possession,” Trump wrote on Truth Social on Monday. “The Governor, very respectfully, understood that, and I will be speaking to him in the near future. He was happy that Tom Homan was going to Minnesota, and so am I!”

Homan, the White House border czar, said during a Thursday briefing that federal immigration operations in Minnesota were undergoing internal changes after Good and Pretti’s deaths. Trump dispatched Homan on Monday.

Minnesota elected officials at the state and local level have called on the Trump administration to withdraw federal immigration officers from the state following Good and Pretti’s death. The tide shifted among some Republicans after the second death, with Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) saying that the credibility of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) “are at stake,” and two other senators suggesting that DHS Secretary Kristi Noem should be removed from office.

Senate Democrats are threatening to block a government-spending bill that would fund DHS, increasing the chances for a partial government shutdown. Some Senate Republicans are willing to leave out the DHS funding bill for now, but House Republicans warn that any changes to the bill would not pass in the House.

data.title: Campaign finance corruption in New York: Leftist attack on political speech continues
data.description:
This week marks 50 years since the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in the landmark political speech case of Buckley v. Valeo.  

This case established that expenditures by political candidates, campaigns and committees constitute political speech protected by the First Amendment, and therefore could not be subject to spending limits.

Similarly, the court held that contributions to campaigns, although not given as broad of protection as expenditures, could only be capped for the limited purposes of combatting corruption or the appearance of corruption. Contribution limits to “level the playing field” for underfunded candidates, according to the Supreme Court, violated the First Amendment.

In the five decades since the Buckley decision, the Supreme Court has consistently upheld the rights of those who seek to speak about candidates, politics and public policy. Later decisions by the high court, such as Citizens United, and lower court decisions like Speechnow.org v. Federal Election Commission, continued the trend toward increased constitutional protection for political speech, regardless of the identity or funder of the speech. 

Progressives continue their hostility to these decisions, as well as the larger framework established by the Buckley decision, to this day. But although progressive politicians constantly carp about Buckley, Citizens United and money in politics generally, they haven’t been shy in using the few remaining tools at their disposal to undermine the political speech protections established by the courts.   

New York, more than any other state, has served as the left’s laboratory for end-runs around the free speech regime established by Buckley. These efforts, however, have rarely accomplished — and likely won’t accomplish —  the goals they initially set out to achieve.

For the progressive left in the Empire State, the crown jewels of these “reforms” are New York’s generous public campaign finance programs. New York City’s program was established in 1988, but New York State’s is a more recent innovation, quietly slipped into a budget bill in the early days of the COVID-19 lockdown. It had its trial run in the 2024 state legislative races, and in 2026, it will also be available for statewide candidates. 

Although the program has only had one opportunity to prove itself, it is, so far, falling short of what its backers promised.

One participant, an Assembly candidate from Queens, has been charged federally with orchestrating a fraudulent donor scheme to illegally obtain more than $150,000 in public matching funds. Another 2024 participant, a state Senate candidate from Central New York, is alleged to have used homeless people as straw donors to qualify. The Senate candidate’s campaign allegedly traded gift cards for campaign donations.

But the state program’s problems go beyond scams to illegally receive public matching funds. What the program permits is often just as odious. In the 2024 cycle, dozens of state Senate candidates and more than 100 Assembly candidates were able to claim public matching funds for donations made by their spouses and family members. While this might be legal, it’s hard to see it as anything more than a way for candidates, consultants and campaign workers to game the system for more public campaign dollars. And it certainly doesn’t fit with the stated intentions of the progressive activists who spent years advocating for New York state to adopt a public campaign finance program. 

Even the Democratic state commissioners who oversee the state public finance program have been accused of improperly tinkering with the system to provide a benefit for their party’s candidates. After the 2024 election concluded, the body that administers the program, the Public Campaign Finance Board, on a party-line vote approved a resolution retroactively allowing candidates in the program to transfer public funds without penalty to party committees. These transfers were, in some circumstances, over $100,000.

The much older New York City public finance program also has a checkered history. For instance, the campaign of 2013 New York City mayoral candidate John Liu was embroiled in a scandal to obtain public matching funds through fraudulent contributions. Liu, who is now a New York state senator from Queens, was never charged with a crime, but his campaign was fined by the New York City Campaign Finance Board. Other members of Liu’s campaign team were tried and convicted of federal wire fraud, obstruction of justice, and other crimes associated with their involvement in the scheme. 

More recently, a scandal surrounding the New York City public finance program reached all the way into the office of New York’s lieutenant governor. Brian Benjamin, under Gov. Kathy Hochul’s first lieutenant governor, was charged by federal prosecutors for his alleged involvement in a fraudulent donation scheme to benefit his campaign for New York City comptroller. Benjamin, who resigned immediately after being charged, eventually saw the charges against him dropped, but this occurred only after a key witness in the case died.  

It’s hard to see how the Empire State’s “reforms” have been a success. New Yorkers continue to have a low opinion of their state government, and persistent pessimism in the state’s direction. Corruption in government continues, and the public funding of elections has, almost certainly, opened the door for even more corruption.  

New York’s progressives spent years searching for workarounds to Buckley. They found their antidote in the state and city public campaign finance programs.

But as the 50th anniversary of Buckley approaches, and as the Supreme Court signals a willingness to expand its political speech protections in upcoming decisions, New York’s progressive left might pause to ask whether their counters to Buckley have accomplished anything beyond enriching political consultants and creating new opportunities for the corruption they claim to oppose. 

Joe Burns is a partner with the Holtzman Vogel law firm, with a focus on election cases in New York State. He previously served as deputy director of election operations at the New York State Board of Elections.
